FT_IC1.evidence: U.S. ABCD Study, baseline ages 9-10 (2016-2018), 2-year follow-up (2018-2020); n=9,208 from 21 U.S. recruitment sites

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical prospective cohort analysis with logistic regression examining baseline screen time and subsequent OCD diagnosis

FT_IC6.evidence: Published peer-reviewed article in Journal of Adolescent Health, 2023

FT_IC2.evidence: Qualifying SM/phone variable: screen time modalities (TV, videos, video games, texting, video chat, social media) measured as baseline exposure (IV). Other side: OCD diagnosis based on KSADS-5 at 2-year follow-up (DV). OCD is individual-trait-level mental health outcome

FT_IC3.evidence: Multiple quantitative results reported: total screen time aOR 1.05 (95% CI 1.01-1.09); video games aOR 1.15 (95% CI 1.03-1.28); watching videos aOR 1.11 (95% CI 1.01-1.23)

FT_IC4.evidence: Inferential analysis with numeric results for SM-outcome relationship; adjusted odds ratios with confidence intervals in regression models

FT_EC7_reason: Longitudinal design but screen time is IV (predictor), not DV-only; therefore EC7 does not apply
notes: Strong prospective evidence; specific screen modalities (video games and watching videos) showed strongest associations with OCD

FT_IC1.evidence: U.S. ABCD Study, Year 2 assessment (2018-2020), early adolescents aged 10-14 (48.8% female); n=10,280

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical cross-sectional analysis; regression analyses examined associations between bedtime screen use and sleep outcomes

FT_IC6.evidence: Published peer-reviewed article in Sleep Health, 2023

FT_IC2.evidence: Qualifying SM/phone variables: bedtime screen activities (streaming movies/TV, video games, music, phone/text, social media, chat rooms, Internet, computer/laptop, reading) and device presence/phone ringer settings as IV. Other side: sleep outcomes (trouble falling/staying asleep, overall sleep disturbance) as DV. Sleep is individual-trait-level health outcome

FT_IC3.evidence: Multiple quantitative results reported: TV/streaming ARR 1.15 (95% CI 1.09-1.21); video games ARR 1.22 (95% CI 1.14-1.29); music ARR 1.20 (95% CI 1.14-1.25); phone/text ARR 1.16 (95% CI 1.09-1.23); social media ARR 1.16 (95% CI 1.10-1.23); chat rooms ARR 1.27 (95% CI 1.17-1.39)

FT_IC4.evidence: Inferential modified Poisson regression analysis with numeric results (adjusted risk ratios with confidence intervals) for multiple bedtime screen behaviors

FT_EC7_reason: Cross-sectional design; EC7 only applies to longitudinal studies. Therefore EC7 does not apply
notes: Comprehensive examination of specific bedtime screen behaviors; strong associations across multiple screen activity types

FT_IC1.evidence: U.S. ABCD Study, baseline ages 9-11 (2016-2018), 1-year follow-up (2017-2019); N=11,875; data from ABCD 3.0 release

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical prospective cohort analysis; modified Poisson regression examining baseline screen time and 1-year conduct/oppositional defiant disorder outcomes

FT_IC6.evidence: Published peer-reviewed article in Journal of Child Psychology and Psychiatry, 2023

FT_IC2.evidence: Qualifying SM/phone variables: screen time modalities (total screen, social media, video chat, texting, TV/movies, video games) measured at baseline as IV. Other side: conduct disorder and oppositional defiant disorder diagnosed via KSADS-5 at 1-year follow-up (DV). Both are individual-trait-level behavioral/psychiatric outcomes

FT_IC3.evidence: Multiple quantitative results reported: total screen time PR 1.07 (95% CI 1.03-1.11) for conduct, PR 1.05 (95% CI 1.03-1.08) for ODD; social media PR 1.62 (95% CI 1.39-1.87) for conduct; video chat PR 1.21 (95% CI 1.06-1.37); texting PR 1.19 (95% CI 1.07-1.33); TV/movies PR 1.17 (95% CI 1.10-1.25); video games PR 1.14 (95% CI 1.07-1.21) for ODD

FT_IC4.evidence: Inferential modified Poisson regression analysis with numeric prevalence ratios (with confidence intervals) reported for multiple screen modalities

FT_EC7_reason: Longitudinal design but screen time is IV (predictor), not DV-only; therefore EC7 does not apply
notes: Prospective analysis of contemporary screen modalities; strongest association between social media and conduct disorder
